ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic curry rice dish entry; no specific code exists for beef and triple cheese Japanese curry rice. FOODON_00002809 used as closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based dish with meat and sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Japanese beef cheese curry rice. A0FJP (rice-based dish with meat and sauce) used as closest defensible category anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of beef and triple cheese curry rice weighs approximately 350g, comprising steamed white rice topped with Japanese curry sauce, braised beef, and a triple cheese blend.

nutrient_source.title: Beef & Triple Cheese Curry Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef & Triple Cheese Curry Rice (record G00010215). All per_100g core values preserved from the canonical HPB record. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (beef curry rice with cheese) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for Japanese curry rice (white rice with curry sauce, GI ~54)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Beef & Triple Cheese Curry Rice. GI of 54 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for Japanese curry rice (white rice with curry sauce), which reports GI ~54. The addition of beef protein and cheese fat is expected to moderately lower the effective GI relative to plain white rice (GI ~72). GL per serving calculated as: (54 × 25.19g carbohydrate per 100g × 350g serving) / 100 ≈ 36.4g. GI 54 is classified as low per University of Sydney GI Database classification (low <55, medium 55–70, high >70).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g; cumulative sodium risk from curry sauce and cheese toppings
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 231 mg/100g is in the borderline range; a 350g serving delivers approximately 809 mg sodium, representing 35–40% of the 2000 mg daily limit recommended by the DASH diet, and patients with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.19 g/100g with low GI (~54); dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g moderates glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: A 350g serving provides approximately 88g of carbohydrate with a glycaemic load of ~36, which is high for a single meal; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 200–250g, consider substituting white rice with brown or basmati rice to lower GI, and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and triple cheese (~1.8 g/100g); sodium 231 mg/100g; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100g in HPB source
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of red meat (beef) and triple cheese contributes saturated fat estimated at ~1.8 g/100g; a 350g serving delivers approximately 6.3g saturated fat, approaching the 7–10g daily limit for cardiovascular risk management, and patients with CVD should limit portion frequency, choose leaner beef cuts, and reduce or omit the cheese topping.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is high in arachidonic acid (omega-6); triple cheese adds saturated fat; combination is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Beef is a primary dietary trigger for rheumatoid arthritis due to its high arachidonic acid content, which amplifies inflammatory cytokine pathways; individuals with RA should consider substituting beef with chicken or tofu, reducing or omitting the triple cheese topping, and being aware that potato (a nightshade vegetable present in Japanese curry) may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef and Triple Cheese Curry Rice is a Japanese-style curry rice dish served in Singapore, featuring steamed short-grain white rice topped with a rich Japanese curry sauce, braised beef, and a triple cheese blend. Japanese curry (karē raisu) is characteristically milder, sweeter, and thicker than South Asian curries, typically made from a roux-based curry block containing wheat flour, spices, and often potato, carrot, and onion. The triple cheese topping adds a creamy, savoury layer that melts into the hot curry sauce. The dish has a soft, starchy texture from the rice, a thick and mildly spiced sauce, and a rich umami depth from the beef and cheese combination. Per 100g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, 25.19g carbohydrate, 3.7g dietary fibre, and 231mg sodium. The high dietary fibre content (3.7g/100g) is notable for a rice-based dish and is attributable to the vegetable components in the curry. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium content, moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to low GI and carbohydrate load, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from beef and cheese. It is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of red meat. This dish is commonly served as a lunch or dinner item in Japanese curry restaurants and food courts in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for this exact dish (G00010215) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogy and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for Japanese curry rice and documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.8 g/100g) is an analogous estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central composite data for beef curry rice with cheese, as the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. This value should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite Japanese curry rice dish are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16; limited published data for traditional preparation of this specific dish variant. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 mg/100g in the HPB source; this is confirmed zero per the HPB canonical record, though it is nutritionally unexpected for a beef and cheese dish and may reflect a reporting limitation of the HPB entry — users should treat this value with caution. 4. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for Japanese curry rice (white rice with curry sauce); no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; the dish contains beef and cheese and halal certification depends on the specific restaurant or preparation. 6. Local-language names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are provided as translational equivalents for Singapore-market retrieval, reflecting the dish's availability in Singapore food courts; the dish is Japanese in cuisine origin and the primary mandatory local-language name is Japanese.
